coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor using single self-enhanced nanoluminophores for the detection of spermine
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiangmin
Wu, Yang
Bian, Huifang
Peng, Zhengdong
Liu, Yongmei
Yin, Yongde
Du, Jie
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric electrochemiluminescence strategy using a single luminophore for accurate and sensitive biomolecule detection could be immensely valuable in bioanalysis. Herein, an ultrasensitive ratiometric electrochemiluminescence sensing system was fabricated using a self-enhanced luminophore with dual-signal emission for the detection of spermine. A nanocomposite was synthesized by the covalent attachment of N, N-diisopropylethylenediamine onto glutathione-protected Au–Ag bimetallic nanoclusters (DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs). The nanocomposite exhibited efficient intra-cluster charge transfer to produce strong anodic self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence emission at 0.8 V without external co-reactants. Interestingly, the DPEA@GSH@Au–Ag BNCs exhibited cathodic electrochemiluminescence emission upon the addition of the co-reactant potassium persulfate at −1.6 V, exhibiting stable and efficient dual-signal electrochemiluminescence emission features at a continuous potential window of −1.75 to 1.2 V. Thus, they were used to fabricate a single-luminophore electrochemiluminescence sensor with dual emission. The cathodic emission of the biosensor gradually increased with increasing concentrations of spermine, whereas the anodic electrochemiluminescence intensity remained almost constant, enabling the ratiometric detection of spermine. The fabricated biosensor, with an internal standard, significantly improved the accuracy and reliability of spermine detection in a wide concentration range of 0.85 pM–100 μM, with a low limit of detection of 0.12 pM (S/N = 3) under optimum conditions. This single-luminophore electrochemiluminescence sensing system could be used for the detection of spermine and could guide the construction of ratiometric electrochemiluminescence sensors in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric electrochemiluminescence
Self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence
Nanoluminophores
Au–Ag bimetallic Nanoclusters
Spermine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the determination of SPM in fetal bovine serum samples
Electrochemical and ECL behavior of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs
Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor for SPM

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21565020 , 22164018 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs. B) UV–visible spectra and C) Fourier transform infrared spectra of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curves a) and GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curves b). D) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curves a) and DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curves b) and N, N-diisopropylethylenediamine (DPEA) and GSH@Au/Ag BNCs mixed curves (curves c). Inset in (A): high-resolution TEM of single DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs.

A) Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) potential and B) the corresponding cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of (a) phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), (b) GSH@Au/Ag BNCs, (c) DPEA (10 mM), (d) DPEA (10 mM) + GSH@Au/Ag BNCs, and (e) DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs by potential scans in the range of 0–1.2 V. C) PL emission spectra of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (green line) and ECL spectroscopy (red line). D) A schematic diagram of the possible self-enhanced ECL reaction pathway of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

A) ECL potential and B) the corresponding CV curves of (a) PBS, (b) K2S2O8, (c) DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs, and (d) DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs + K2S2O8 by potential scan from 0 to −1.7 V. C) The ECL potential of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (a) with 0.01 M K2S2O8 and (b) without K2S2O8 by potential scans in the range of 1.2 to −1.7 V. D) ECL responses of the DPEA-Au/Ag BNCs toward 0, 10 pM, and 10 nM spermine (SPM) containing 0.01 M K2S2O8. Inset in A): the cathodic ECL spectroscopy of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs with 0.01 M K2S2O8. Inset in B): a schematic diagram of the possible principle of the cathodic ECL of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs.

A) The ECL response of the biosensor toward SPM concentrations from 0 to 100 μM. (B) The relationship between SPM concentration and I
r . Inset in B): the linear calibration curve of the biosensor for SPM detection. Error bars have been indicated based on results obtained after performing each experiment thrice (n = 3).

A) The selectivity of the biosensor to different interfering substances. B) The stability of the ECL biosensor during 10 consecutive CV scans. The reproducibility of the ECL biosensor by an C) intra-assay and D) inter-assay. Error bars have been indicated based on results obtained after performing each experiment thrice (n = 3).
A comparison of SPM detection methods.
Recovery tests for SPM using fetal bovine serum (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: An average value of three measurements.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor using single self-enhanced nanoluminophores for the detection of spermine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, Gansu, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, Gansu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, Gansu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, Gansu, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Life Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, Gansu, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric electrochemiluminescence strategy using a single luminophore for accurate and sensitive biomolecule detection could be immensely valuable in bioanalysis. Herein, an ultrasensitive ratiometric electrochemiluminescence sensing system was fabricated using a self-enhanced luminophore with dual-signal emission for the detection of spermine. A nanocomposite was synthesized by the covalent attachment of N, N-diisopropylethylenediamine onto glutathione-protected Au–Ag bimetallic nanoclusters (DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs). The nanocomposite exhibited efficient intra-cluster charge transfer to produce strong anodic self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence emission at 0.8 V without external co-reactants. Interestingly, the DPEA@GSH@Au–Ag BNCs exhibited cathodic electrochemiluminescence emission upon the addition of the co-reactant potassium persulfate at −1.6 V, exhibiting stable and efficient dual-signal electrochemiluminescence emission features at a continuous potential window of −1.75 to 1.2 V. Thus, they were used to fabricate a single-luminophore electrochemiluminescence sensor with dual emission. The cathodic emission of the biosensor gradually increased with increasing concentrations of spermine, whereas the anodic electrochemiluminescence intensity remained almost constant, enabling the ratiometric detection of spermine. The fabricated biosensor, with an internal standard, significantly improved the accuracy and reliability of spermine detection in a wide concentration range of 0.85 pM–100 μM, with a low limit of detection of 0.12 pM (S/N = 3) under optimum conditions. This single-luminophore electrochemiluminescence sensing system could be used for the detection of spermine and could guide the construction of ratiometric electrochemiluminescence sensors in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an advantageous analytical technique combining electrochemical and spectroscopic methods with negligible background signals, even without an extra light source [1]. It holds great promise for clinical diagnosis, food safety, environmental inspection, etc. A variety of ECL biosensors have been constructed to detect nucleic acids [2,3], proteins, small biological molecules [4], and different types of ECL luminophores, including Ru (bpy)3 2+, quantum dots (QDs), metal-organic frameworks, and metal nanomaterials [5–9]. Nanomaterial-based luminophores (nanoluminophores) can be used to fabricate a diverse range of ECL biosensors because of their unique properties such as tunable photochemical features, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and easy labeling [10–13]. In these ECL biosensors, the sensing signal is commonly based on ECL intensity changes, which originate from the response of the luminophore/co-reactant to the analyte. Although these biosensors have many advantages, they can exhibit false negative or false positive results because the emission of nanoluminophores is easily affected by environmental factors, especially in complex biological samples.

Ratiometric ECL is a useful strategy to eliminate false results, which normalizes internal and external interference through self-calibration of two ECL signals, improving the accuracy and repeatability of detection [4,14]. Two main mechanisms, dual-wavelength and dual-potential are currently used for designing ratiometric ECL biosensors [15,16]. Generally, a dual-wavelength ECL sensor requires a pair of donor-acceptor ECL resonance energy transfers (ECL-RET) for ratio detection using the emission intensity of the donor and acceptor at two wavelengths [17,18]. The ECL-RET system relies on the overlapping emission spectrum of the donor and absorption spectrum of the acceptor, generating high emission efficiency and detection sensitivity; however, it is challenging to form a matched high-performance ECL donor-acceptor pair [19,20]. In contrast, a dual-potential ECL sensor requires only a pair of anodic-cathodic emitters with independent ECL responses, with the ratio signal of the sensor measured through potential scanning [21,22]. Zhang et al. reported a potential-resolved ECL sensor with CdS nanocrystals (NCs) as cathodic emitters and luminol-gold nanoparticles (L-Au NPs) as anodic emitters for the ratio detection of telomerase [23]. Another ratiometric ECL biosensor was reported by Shang et al., with gold@carbon dots (Au@CDs)/potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) as the cathodic ECL unit and Au@luminol/H2O2 as the anodic ECL unit [24]. A major limitation of these sensors is the composition of the measurement system, consisting of multiple emitters and co-reactants, which is not conducive to practical applications. To overcome this limitation, some single-luminophore ECL sensors possessing anodic/cathodic emission have been proposed [25–27]. Xia and co-workers reported a dual-potential ECL emitter based on a single graphene QD luminophore using two different co-reactants, K2S2O8 and Na2SO3, to detect human IgG [28]. Wang et al. also developed a ratiometric ECL immunosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) using a single luminophore of graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4), generating the cathodic ECL with a K2S2O8 co-reactant and anodic ECL with a triethanolamine co-reactant [29]. Although these studies have reduced the complexity of the detection solution, further simplification, such as the use of fewer co-reactants, could be very useful.

Self-enhanced or co-reactant-free ECL, a recently developed ECL strategy, involves ECL emitters that contain the luminophore and co-reactive group in a single molecular structure or composite, generating ECL emission without external co-reactants [9,30–32]. Self-enhanced ECL exhibits high emission efficiency and stability; intramolecular reaction between the luminophore and the co-reactive group, with an effectively shorter electronic transmission distance, effectively reduces background interference [33–35]. Yuan et al. developed several self-enhanced ECL systems based on ruthenium (II) complex-linked co-reactive groups, which have been applied to fabricate highly sensitive sensors [36–38]. In addition to these traditional ECL reagents, nanoluminophores, such as, Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and carbon nanomaterials, have been used to develop self-enhanced ECL systems [11,33,39]. Recently, Wang et al. fabricated a self-enhanced ECL system based on nanoluminophores, composed of lipoic acid-stabilized Au NCs and N,N-dimethylethylenediamine with improved ECL efficiency and no addition of co-reactants [11]. Yang et al. reported a self-enhanced ECL system based on covalently linked thioctic acid-stabilized Au NCs and N,N-diisopropylethylenediamine (DPEA), constructing a co-reactant free ECL sensor [40]. Thus, self-enhanced ECL avoids the addition of co-reactants into the test solution, simplifying the sensor design.

Spermine (SPM), a biogenic aliphatic tetraamine, is present in the fluids and cells of many living organisms, and some studies have suggested that SPM can interact with negatively charged macromolecules, such as, nucleic acids, proteins, membranes, and other regulatory molecules needed for transcription, enzymatic activity, and overall cell functioning. SPM levels correlate with cell proliferation and differentiation, and can be used as biomarkers for early cancer diagnosis [41,42]. Additionally, SPM is a key trigger factor in food toxicity, and a substantial indicator of food quality and safety [43,44]. Several analytical techniques for the determination of SPM, such as, high-performance liquid chromatography [45], chromatography-mass spectrometry [46], fluorescence spectroscopy [47], and capillary electrophoresis [48] have been developed. Despite their advantages, these methods require tedious sampling equipment, expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, and highly skilled staff, significantly limiting their practical application [49]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new analytical methods for the simple, sensitive, and rapid determination of SPM.

In this study, a ratiometric biosensor based on self-enhanced ECL was fabricated for SPM detection. Glutathione (GSH)-protected Au–Ag BNCs (GSH@Au–Ag BNCs) rich in carboxyl groups were prepared via the hydrothermal method and used as basic ECL nanoluminophores [50]. Subsequently, the DPEA co-reactant was functionalized onto the GSH@Au/Ag BNCs via covalent linking, forming co-reactant–embedded ECL nanoluminophores, synthesizing a new nanocomposite (DPEA-GSH@Au–Ag BNC). The as-prepared DPEA-GSH@Au–Ag BNCs emitted a strong and stable anodic self-enhanced ECL that was attributed to the effective intra-cluster reaction between Au/Ag and DPEA; the reduced electron transfer distance of DPEA-GSH@Au–Ag BNCs improved ECL efficiency compared to that of GSH@Au–Ag BNCs. Notably, DPEA-GSH@Au–Ag BNCs not only retained the cathode ECL emission properties of GSH@Au–Ag BNCs but also emitted dual-signals (both cathode and anode ECL simultaneously) on potential scanning from −1.75 to 1.2 V in the presence of K2S2O8. Thus, the co-reactant–embedded nanoemitter was used to fabricate an ECL biosensor using chitosan as a matrix. This sensor did not require multiple co-reactants in the detection solution; the cathodic ECL emission gradually increased with increasing concentrations of SPM, whereas the anodic ECL remained almost constant. Thus, it could be used for the sensitive ratiometric detection of SPM in real sample. The proposed sensing strategy not only provides a new assay protocol for the detection of SPM but also broadens the design pathway for ratiometric ECL sensor fabrication.
The reagents and apparatus required for the experiments and synthesis and the optimization of the experimental conditions are described in the Supporting Information.

Au/Ag bimetallic nanoclusters protected by glutathione (GSH@Au/Ag BNCs), synthesized using a previously published procedure, were used as base nanoluminophores [50]. Self-enhanced DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were subsequently synthesized according to a previously reported method with some modifications [40]. EDC (1-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride) (0.0383 g) was added to 5 mL of 10 mg/mL GSH@Au/Ag BNC with stirring for 2 h at 25 °C, followed by the addition of 5.5 M DPEA (35 μL) to the reaction mixture for 8 h. The synthesized DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were filtered through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 3500 Da) in ultrapure water for 48 h and stored at −4 °C until use.

Bare GCE was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry, ultrasonicated in water and ethanol, and dried with high-purity N2 at room temperature. Subsequently, 1 mg/mL DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNC solution (5 μL) was dropped on the treated GCE. The DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNC-modified electrode was then dried at 37 °C. Finally, 0.5% CS was modified onto the surface of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNC/GCE by electrodeposition (−0.95 V; 120 s) and dried at room temperature, fabricating the biosensor. It was stored at −4 °C for characterization and further use. Other modified electrodes were prepared using the same procedure. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were used to characterize the different modification stages of the electrode surface to confirm the successful formation of the biosensor (Fig. S9, S10).

Electrochemical and ECL measurements were performed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 8.0, 0.1 M) with or without K2S2O8 (0.01 M). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range of −1.7 to 1.2 V at 0.1 V s−1 scan rate was used to record the ECL signals, using an ECL analyzer, with the voltage of photomultiplier tube set at 700 V. The ECL responses of CV, on addition of different concentrations of SPM into PBS, using the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs/GCE as the working electrode, were recorded for SPM determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the determination of SPM in fetal bovine serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fetal bovine serum samples were diluted 20-fold with PBS (0.1 M, pH 8.0), and SPM (at concentrations of 10 nM, 100 nM and 1 μM) was added to them. The proposed method and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method were both used to calculate the recovery. All experiments were repeated thrice.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs. As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 1 A), a large number of evenly dispersed DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were observed, with sizes primarily distributed in the range of 1.97–4.13 nm, with a mean diameter of 3.24 nm. As shown in the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1A, inset), the crystal structure contained a clear lattice fringe of 0.24 nm and 0.22 nm, consistent with that of Au/Ag BNCs (Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information) [51]. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy mapping analysis was used to characterize the elemental distribution of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (Fig. S2), confirming the presence of Au and Ag. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were used to investigate the crosslinking between the GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and DPEA. As shown in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1B), a broader absorption peak was observed at approximately 420 nm for the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curve a) than that observed for the GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curve b), indicating the formation of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs through a coupling reaction. Differences between the characteristic vibrations of –CO–NH- at 1189 and 3199 cm−1 for the GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were observed in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1C), indicating dependence of the crosslinking on covalent interactions between COO- (in the GSH ligand of the BNCs) and DPEA [52]. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 1D, the maximum photoluminescence (PL) emission wavelength of the GSH@Au/Ag BNCs was observed at 630 nm (curve a) that was slightly red shifted to 654 nm after crosslinking with DPEA (curve b); a simple mixture of DPEA and GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curve c) did not exhibit a red shift. Thus, the crosslinking of DPEA modified the electronic structure of the surface BNCs, causing a red shift of the PL emission [53]. Therefore, the fabrication of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs via the proposed covalent linking procedure was unambiguously confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and ECL behavior of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL properties of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were first investigated in PBS without adding any co-reactants. Fig. 2 A shows the ECL-voltage curve of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs in the potential range of 0–1.20 V. A distinct anodic ECL emission of approximately 1542 a.u. Was observed at 0.89 V (curve e), whereas negligible ECL emission was observed for bare GCE (curve a). GSH@Au/Ag BNCs + DPEA was used for comparison under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2A, the ECL emission of GSH@Au/Ag BNCs + DPEA (curve d) was weak, whereas those of GSH@Au/Ag BNCs (curve b) and DPEA (curve c) were negligible, indicating that covalently linking GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and the co-reactant DPEA caused self-enhanced ECL activity. To elucidate the self-enhanced ECL emission process, the ECL emission spectra and corresponding CV behavior of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were examined. The ECL emission spectra were recorded using an optical filter. As shown in Fig. 2C, the peak of the ECL emission spectrum appeared at 750 nm, exhibiting a significant red shift compared to the PL peak (654 nm), indicating that ECL emission might be primarily dependent on the sensitive surface-state transitions of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs [52,54]. The CV spectrum (Fig. 2B) of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs indicated an irreversible oxidation peak at approximately 0.88 V (curve e). GSH@Au/Ag BNCs + DPEA also exhibited oxidation processes in a comparable potential range (curve d), with lower peak currents than those of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs. The oxidation peak of DPEA was observed at approximately 0.80 V (curve c) and used as a control. Thus, ECL was generated via charge transfer between electrochemically oxidized BNCs and oxidized DPEA, which was significantly enhanced by the covalent coupling of DPEA and GSH@Au/Ag BNCs, to generate maximum ECL intensity at 0.89 V, in agreement with previous reports on the self-enhanced behavior of Au NCs [11,40]. A possible self-enhanced ECL reaction pathway could include the following four key steps (Fig. 2D). Anodization of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and the DPEA•+-GSH@Au/Ag+ BNCs, followed by the conversion of the DPEA•+-GSH@Au/Ag+ BNCs into the intermediate DPEA•-GSH@Au/Ag+ BNCs after deprotonation, to form the excited-state DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag* BNCs via electron transfer and energy transmission of the intra-cluster DPEA and Au/Ag. Subsequently, the return of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag* BNCs to the low-energy-state DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs, releasing photons, to complete the ECL emission.

Some Au/Ag BNCs with K2S2O8 as a co-reactant exhibit significant ECL emission under negative potential [55]. Therefore, the cathodic ECL behavior of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs was investigated via CV with a potential scan from 0 to −1.75 V. A strong cathodic ECL peak at approximately −1.6 V was observed in the presence of 0.01 M K2S2O8 (Fig. 3 A, curve d), whereas no definite ECL emissions were observed in the absence of K2S2O8 (curve c) and for K2S2O8 alone (curve b), indicating cathodic ECL activity by the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs. The mechanism for ECL emission from the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs + K2S2O8 system could be similar to the Au/Ag BNC system, as reported during evaluation of the ECL emission and CV spectra. A schematic illustration of the cathodic ECL process is shown in the inset of Fig. 3B [10].

For a potential-resolved ratiometric ECL sensing platform, two ECL emissions require integration into one system. Thus, to confirm the feasibility of dual ECL emissions, the ECL responses of DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were examined with a potential scan from −1.75 to 1.20 V. Cathodic and anodic emissions of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs were simultaneously obtained in the presence K2S2O8 at approximately −1.6 and 0.89 V (curve a) (Fig. 3C). The anodic emission profile with and without K2S2O8 (curve b) exhibited similar shapes qualitatively, indicating that the self-enhanced anodic and cathodic emissions were independent of the ECL responses. Thus, a potential-resolved ECL sensing platform could be constructed by only using the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs.

To verify feasibility, the effect of SPM on both the anodic and cathodic ECL intensities was examined, as shown in Fig. 3D. In the presence of SPM, the anodic ECL (ECL
a ) signal changed negligibly, whereas the cathodic ECL (ECL
c ) intensity markedly increased, indicating the applicability of this system for the ratiometric detection of SPM. The ECL response mechanism to SPM could be as follows. DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and S2O8 2− are simultaneously reduced to DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag•-, SO4 2−, and SO4 •- on scanning from 0 to −1.7 V, followed by the reaction of the strong oxidant SO4 •- with DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag•- to form the excited-state DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag*. Finally, DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag* emits light at 620 nm, returning to the ground state. On the addition of SPM, however, it is reduced to SPM* and reacts with SO4 2− to produce more SO4 •-. Thus, the ECL signal of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs increased on increasing the SPM concentration, summarized by the following [56]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor for SPM
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the excellent dual ECL performance of the DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNCs and the increment of cathodic ECL by SPM, a ratiometric ECL biosensor for the detection of SPM was fabricated using chitosan as the matrix. Under the optimized conditions (details in the Supporting Information), different concentrations of SPM were determined by the biosensor in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 8.0) containing 0.01 M K2S2O8. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the cathodic ECL intensity (I
c ) increased with increasing the SPM concentration, whereas the anodic ECL intensity (I
a ) remained almost unchanged. Considering the relative intensity change of I
r (
I r
= Δ
I c
/
I a
,
Δ
I c
=
I c
−
I c 0
) as a function of the SPM concentration, a linear relationship between I
r and the logarithm of SPM concentration (lgC
SPM ) was obtained in the range of 0.85 pM–100 μM (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation was
I r
= 0.0152 +
1.1980 l g
C
S P M
, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9924. For lower concentration levels of SPM in the range of 0.85 pM–1 nM, the linear regression equation was
I r
= 0.0911 +
1.2059 l g
C
S P M
with a correlation coefficient of 0.9616 (Fig. S11). The limit of detection (LOD) of the established ECL biosensor was calculated to be 0.12 pM, based on 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of the background ECL and S is the slope of the linear trendline in the linear dynamic range). The ratiometric biosensor exhibited a wider linear range and lower detection limit for SPM compared to that of previously used sensors (Table 1 ).

The selectivity of the biosensor was evaluated by comparing the ECL responses of SPM, putrescine (PUT), spermidine (SPD), and cadaverine (CAD). As shown in Fig. 5 A and Fig. S12, the target SPM and mixture solutions exhibited high I
r values, whereas PUT, SPD, and CAD did not cause a significant response, even with 10-fold higher concentrations than SPM (10 nM). Although SPD exhibited a slightly higher response, which could be due to the similarity in the structures of SPD and SPM. However, physiological concentrations of SPD are typically significantly lower than of SPM [57,58], thus, this ECL platform should be capable of detecting SPM in body fluids selectively.

The stability of the ratiometric biosensor was evaluated by consecutive cyclic potential scanning (Fig. 5B). The anodic and cathodic ECL signals remained stable after 10 cycles of scanning at 0.1 V s−1 in the presence of 10 nM SPM, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 2.80% and 2.12%, respectively, indicating high stability of the biosensor.

Additionally, the reproducibility of the ECL biosensor was evaluated. Five biosensors prepared in the same batch were used to perform an intra-assay (Fig. 5C), whereby the cathodic and anodic ECL signals for the detection of 10 nM SPM did not show any significant changes. The calculated RSDs were 4.67% (anodic) and 3.42% (cathodic). An inter-assay was evaluated using five ECL biosensors from different batches (Fig. 5C), with RSDs of 4.13% (anodic) and 3.07% (cathodic). Thus, the biosensor exhibited acceptable reproducibility.

To evaluate the application potential of the proposed ECL biosensor in real sample analysis, different concentrations of SPM were added to 20-fold diluted fetal bovine serum samples. Recovery values were calculated using the standard addition method (Table 2 ). The SPM recovery of the fabricated biosensor ranged from 102.1 to 104.7%, with RSDs in the range of 0.76–2.9%. In contrast, the recovery of SPM via HPLC was in the range of 98.2–105.8%, with RSDs in the range of 0.37–1.41%. Thus, the ratiometric method has immense potential for the efficient detection of SPM in real samples.

In this study, DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNC was used as a single luminophore to fabricate a ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor for SPM determination. DPEA-GSH@Au/Ag BNC was synthesized by the facile chemical crosslinking between the GSH@Au/Ag BNC luminophore and DPEA co-reactant that facilitated electron transfer between them, enhancing ECL efficiency and enabling self-enhanced ECL. Interestingly, the fabricated biosensor exhibited the unique property of dual ECL emission in a single co-reactant (K2S2O8) system. The addition of SPM enhanced the cathodic ECL intensity of the biosensor further, facilitating SPM estimation by the ratio mode with a very low LOD (0.12 pM). This single-luminophore-based ECL ratio mode could overcome the limitations of using multiple luminophores, simplify the biosensor construction process, with immense applications in bioanalysis.

Jiangmin Wu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yang Wu: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Huifang Bian: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Zhengdong Peng: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yongmei Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Yongde Yin: Validation, Formal analysis. Jie Du: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xiaoquan Lu<span class = "privatefont">：;Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21565020, 22164018).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tu, S. Sun, Y. Xu, A novel self-assembled platform for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of spermine, Chem. Commun. 52(5) (2016) 1040-1043.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Rawat, J.R. Bhamore, R.K. Singhal, S.K. Kailasa, Microwave assisted synthesis of tyrosine protected gold nanoparticles for dual (colorimetric and fluorimetric) detection of spermine and spermidine in biological samples, Biosens. Bioelectron. 88 (2017) 71-77.
